quotes.verbatim_quote:
When skipping strings the same rule applies: When going to a higher string use a
          down-stroke; when going to a lower string, use an up-stroke.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Extends the book's core directional-stroke rule from adjacent strings to non-adjacent strings, the chapter's organizing principle.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Apply this exercise to strings (4 and 2), (5 and 3) and (6 and 4). Use any notes you like.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Standard transposition instruction that turns each tabbed exercise into a systematic drill across all skip-pair combinations.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This exercise is a bit more difficult. It uses the same exercise
           but with the "in-between-stroke".
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the in-between stroke as the harder variant applied to string skipping, layering technique on top of the basic skip pattern.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Consecutive down-up strokes with a string skip.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names a specific skip pattern that violates the default alternation rule, marking it as a deliberate exception to drill.

quotes.verbatim_quote: START THIS EXERCISE WITH BOTH STROKES, DOWN AND UP
quotes.why_load_bearing: Repeated directive that each skip exercise must be practiced beginning on both down and up strokes, ensuring symmetry.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Consecutive up-dwon strokes with a string skip.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Mirror counterpart to the down-up consecutive pattern, completing the pair of non-alternating skip drills.
Applies string skipping to interval-of-a-sixth voicings, a load-bearing musical application beyond mechanical drilling.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This exercise is very difficult to master. You need to put an accent on the "up-stroke"
     while skipping a string. It uses the "in-between" stroke with a string skip. You should also practice this
     with an alternate stroke. I find that when playing exercises like this it is easier to play them
     with alternating "down" - "up" strokes
quotes.why_load_bearing: Combines the hardest elements (accent placement, in-between stroke, skipping) and gives the author's pragmatic preference for alternate picking in this case.
